intro.why: A real trading book can hold thousands of positions — far too many to model one by one. Mapping solves this by re-expressing every position as exposure to a small set of common risk factors, so portfolio VaR can be computed from factor volatilities and correlations instead of from each instrument individually. This lesson works through that idea for fixed-income portfolios, forwards, swaps, and options, and along the way introduces two ideas that recur throughout the curriculum: the undiversified-vs-diversified VaR distinction (the role of correlation), and how mapping supports stress testing and benchmark-relative (tracking error) VaR.
intro.content:
The seven learning outcomes build outward from a general framework to specific instrument types and applications:

  1. Mapping Principles and Process — the two-step template (identify risk factors, then express each position as exposures to them) that every other LO in this lesson applies.
  2. General and Specific Risk — how the choice of risk factors splits a portfolio's risk into a systematic piece the factors explain and a leftover specific-risk piece they don't.
  3. Fixed-Income Mapping (describing the methods) — the three ways to re-express a bond portfolio as zero-coupon positions: principal, duration, and cash-flow mapping.
  4. Fixed-Income Mapping (computing VaR) — applying those three methods to a worked example, and introducing undiversified vs. diversified VaR.
  5. Stress Testing via Mapping — reusing the cash-flow map to stress-test a portfolio by shocking each mapped factor by its own VaR.
  6. Benchmarking and Tracking Error VaR — comparing a portfolio's mapped cash flows to a benchmark's to measure relative risk.
  7. Mapping Forwards, Swaps, and Options — extending the same machinery to derivatives, which map cleanly (forwards, swaps) or only approximately (options).

Keep an eye on the throughline: every mapping is a trade-off between how much detail about a position is preserved and how tractable the resulting VaR calculation becomes — and the undiversified-vs-diversified gap, driven by correlation, shows up again and again as that detail increases.

los.lo: LO 5.a — Explain the principles underlying VaR mapping and describe the mapping process
los.title: VaR Mapping Principles and Process
los.why: Every other LO in this lesson is an application of the same two-step idea introduced here: find the common risk factors, then express each position as exposures to them. Understanding why mapping is necessary — and the principles that make it valid — is the foundation for everything that follows, from fixed-income mapping to derivatives.
los.keyIdea: Mapping replaces a portfolio's actual holdings with a small set of risk-factor exposures that behave the same way for VaR purposes, making a position-by-position variance-covariance calculation tractable.
los.content:
Why mapping is necessary

A trading book can hold thousands of instruments. Estimating a volatility and a pairwise correlation for every single one would require a number of parameters that explodes combinatorially as the position count grows. Mapping sidesteps this by recognizing that most positions are really just different packages of exposure to the same underlying drivers — interest rates at various maturities, an equity index, an exchange rate, and so on. Once these risk factors are identified, the portfolio's risk can be described with a much smaller set of volatility and correlation estimates.

Mapping is especially valuable when:

  • The portfolio is large and heterogeneous, making position-by-position risk computation impractical.
  • Instruments change through time — e.g., a bond's remaining maturity shortens daily, and mapping lets the risk model continuously re-express its exposure in terms of the current maturity bucket.
  • An instrument is new or illiquid with no price history (e.g., a recent IPO) — the manager instead reasons about which broad factors likely drive its value and maps onto those.

Core principles

  • Mapping lets a risk manager aggregate exposure across a portfolio too large to evaluate position by position.
  • It collapses many similar exposures into one factor — thousands of trades sensitive to the same exchange rate become a single aggregate currency exposure.
  • Risk mapping may diverge from pricing: two instruments that must be priced separately can still be lumped together for risk measurement if they share the same risk drivers.
  • Mapping naturally handles instruments that evolve over time, automatically re-mapping a bond to the point on the yield curve matching its current remaining life.
  • Mapping is the natural tool when no historical data exists for an instrument, since the risk factors it maps to typically do have rich histories.

The two-step process

Step 1 — identify common risk factors. Survey every position and determine which underlying market variables drive its value, looking for factors shared across many positions so the problem's dimensionality shrinks.

Step 2 — express each position as factor exposures. Decompose each position's market value into pieces, each assigned to a risk factor. With several positions and several factors, this forms a grid — rows are positions, columns are risk factors, and each cell is the portion of that position's value exposed to that factor. Summing down each column produces a single aggregate exposure per risk factor — a vector that fully replaces the original list of positions for risk-measurement purposes.

Once reduced to this exposure vector, standard variance-covariance VaR machinery — factor volatilities and a factor correlation matrix — applies directly, regardless of how many instruments originally made up the portfolio.
takeaways: Mapping swaps a portfolio's actual holdings for a small set of risk-factor exposures that behave the same way for VaR purposes.
It is essential for large portfolios, instruments that evolve over time, and new/illiquid instruments with no price history.
Risk mapping can diverge from pricing — positions priced separately can still share a risk factor.
The two-step process (identify factors, then express positions as exposures) is the template every later LO applies to a specific instrument type.

los.lo: LO 5.b — Explain and demonstrate how the mapping process captures general and specific risks
los.title: General and Specific Risk in Mapping
los.why: Every mapping choice trades off accuracy against tractability: the risk factors you keep (general risk) explain part of a position's behavior, and whatever is left over becomes specific risk. Understanding this trade-off is the lens for evaluating any mapping decision later in the lesson — whether mapping bonds to maturities or derivatives to building blocks.
los.keyIdea: Specific risk is not fixed — it is simply whatever the chosen general risk factors fail to explain, and it shrinks as the factor set becomes richer.
los.content:
How many risk factors is enough?

There's no fixed answer — it depends on the portfolio and how much modeling effort the institution will invest. Sometimes one or two factors capture the bulk of a portfolio's risk. Adding more factors generally improves accuracy but adds complexity: more volatilities to estimate and a larger correlation matrix to manage.

General vs. specific risk

The factors retained in a mapping are general (primitive) risk factors — broad, systematic drivers like interest rate levels, equity indices, or major exchange rates. Whatever variation in a position's value is not explained by these factors is specific risk — the unsystematic, position-specific (idiosyncratic) component.

The key relationship: the definition of specific risk depends entirely on how finely general risk is defined. A bond portfolio illustrates this:

  • Using only duration as a factor leaves a large specific-risk component (credit quality, currency, etc. all unexplained).
  • Adding a credit risk factor captures more variance, shrinking specific risk.
  • Adding a currency risk factor shrinks it further.

As the set of general factors becomes richer, specific risk asymptotically shrinks toward zero — though in practice it never fully disappears.

Worked example: mapping a large equity portfolio

Consider a portfolio of 5,000 individual stocks. Each stock's return has a market-wide (systematic) component and a firm-specific component. If every stock were its own risk factor, the model would need a covariance term for every pair:

[5,000 × (5,000 − 1)] / 2 ≈ 12.5 million covariance terms — completely impractical to estimate, store, or invert.

Mapping provides the way out: diversification washes out the firm-specific components, leaving mostly the market-wide ('beta') component. Each stock's market risk can be mapped onto a single common factor — a broad equity index — collapsing millions of covariance terms into a handful of parameters.

The regression behind the mapping

Stock i's sensitivity to the market index is estimated by regressing its return on the index return. The intercept (alpha) is dropped from the risk calculation since it reflects average excess return, not risk. The residual (epsilon) is the firm-specific component, assumed uncorrelated with the market and with other stocks' residuals. Beta is the factor exposure — exactly the 'risk factor exposure' concept from the general mapping framework, applied to equities.

Aggregating and splitting variance

Weighting each stock's beta by its portfolio weight gives the portfolio beta — a single number summarizing the whole portfolio's exposure to the index. The portfolio's total return variance then splits cleanly into a general market risk component (driven by portfolio beta and the index's variance) and a specific risk component (driven by the diversified-but-not-eliminated firm-specific residuals). The index mapping captures the general risk; everything else is specific risk.
los.formula:
R_i = alpha_i + beta_i × R_M + epsilon_i
Portfolio beta = Σ w_i × beta_i
V = (general risk: portfolio beta, Var(R_M)) + (specific risk: residuals)
takeaways: Specific risk is whatever the chosen general risk factors haven't been asked to explain yet — it shrinks as factors get richer, but never reaches zero.
More risk factors → better accuracy but more volatilities and a larger correlation matrix to manage.
A 5,000-stock portfolio would need ~12.5 million covariance terms if each stock were its own factor — mapping to a single index collapses this to a handful of parameters.
Beta from R_i = alpha_i + beta_i×R_M + epsilon_i is the factor exposure; alpha is dropped and epsilon becomes specific risk.

los.lo: LO 5.c — Differentiate among the three methods of mapping fixed-income portfolios
los.title: Fixed-Income Mapping Methods — Principal, Duration, and Cash Flow
los.why: A bond portfolio's risk can be re-expressed as a position in zero-coupon bonds of standard maturities, because the risk system already tracks VaR percentages for those maturities. There are three ways to do this, ranging from crude to precise, and they differ only in how much cash-flow detail they preserve — understanding these differences is the foundation for the computations in LO 5.d and for everything that follows in this lesson.
los.keyIdea: Principal, duration, and cash-flow mapping all replace a bond portfolio with equivalent zero-coupon positions, differing only in how much of the cash-flow timing and size detail is preserved.
los.content:
The shared idea

All three methods replace a bond (or bond portfolio) with an equivalent position — or positions — in zero-coupon bonds of standard maturities, since VaR percentages for zero-coupon bonds at standard maturities are already available from the risk system.

1. Principal mapping (simplest)

Looks only at when principal is repaid and ignores coupons entirely. The whole portfolio is treated as a single cash flow occurring at the portfolio's weighted-average maturity (each bond's maturity weighted by its share of total market value). VaR is read off as the VaR percentage for a zero-coupon bond of that average maturity, applied to total portfolio value. Fast and easy, but it discards a lot of information — two portfolios with very different cash-flow profiles but the same average maturity get identical VaR estimates.

2. Duration mapping (intermediate)

Replaces the portfolio with a single zero-coupon bond whose maturity equals the portfolio's Macaulay duration rather than its average maturity. Since duration accounts for the size and timing of all cash flows, this better approximates interest-rate sensitivity than principal mapping. The complication: duration rarely lines up exactly with a standard zero-coupon maturity, so the VaR percentage typically must be interpolated between two adjacent maturities.

3. Cash flow mapping (most precise)

Decomposes the portfolio into its individual cash flows — every coupon and principal payment at its own maturity date. Each cash flow is discounted to present value, and each present value is mapped onto the zero-coupon risk factor for its own maturity. This preserves the complete cash-flow profile rather than collapsing it to one point, making it the most accurate of the three — but also the most demanding, since it additionally requires the correlations between zero-coupon returns at different maturities to compute a diversified VaR.

Comparing the three

MethodWhat's preservedPrecision
Principal mappingOnly the average timing of principal repaymentLowest
Duration mappingThe portfolio's overall interest-rate sensitivity (duration)Medium
Cash flow mappingThe full timing and size of every cash flow, plus inter-maturity correlationsHighest
takeaways: All three methods replace a bond portfolio with equivalent zero-coupon positions — they differ only in how much cash-flow detail survives.
Principal mapping uses weighted-average maturity and ignores coupons entirely — fastest but crudest.
Duration mapping uses Macaulay duration and usually requires interpolating the VaR percentage between two standard maturities.
Cash flow mapping preserves every individual cash flow and is the most accurate, but requires a full correlation matrix across maturities.

los.lo: LO 5.d — Summarize how to map a fixed-income portfolio into positions of standard instruments, and describe how the standard instruments should be chosen
los.title: Fixed-Income Mapping — Computing VaR and the Diversification Gap
los.why: Applying the three mapping methods to an actual portfolio is what turns the conceptual distinctions from LO 5.c into numbers a risk manager can use — and it's where the undiversified-vs-diversified VaR distinction first appears, a theme that recurs in stress testing, benchmarking, and derivative mapping later in this lesson.
los.keyIdea: Cash flow mapping produces both an undiversified VaR (summing individual VaR contributions, assuming perfect correlation) and a lower diversified VaR (using the actual correlation matrix) — the gap between them is the diversification benefit.
los.content:
Worked example setup

A portfolio holds two par-value bonds, each $100 million face value: Bond 1 (1-year maturity, 3.5% coupon) and Bond 2 (5-year maturity, 5% coupon). Total portfolio market value = $200 million (50% weight each, since both are par bonds). Assume 95% VaR percentages for zero-coupon bonds of maturities 1–5 years are known from the risk system.

Principal mapping calculation

Weighted-average life = 0.50 × 1 + 0.50 × 5 = 3 years.
Using the 3-year zero's VaR% of 1.4841%:
Principal mapping VaR = $200 million × 1.4841% = $2.968 million.
This is crude — it ignores that Bond 2 pays coupons every year for five years.

Duration mapping calculation

Macaulay duration sums each cash flow's time-weighted present value and divides by total present value. The sum of time-weighted present values is $553.69 million, so:
Duration = $553.69m / $200m = 2.768 years.
Since 2.768 falls between the 2-year (VaR% = 0.9868%) and 3-year (VaR% = 1.4841%) maturities, interpolate:
VaR%(2.768) = 0.9868 + (1.4841 − 0.9868) × 0.768 = 1.3687%.
Duration mapping VaR = $200 million × 1.3687% = $2.737 million.
Lower than principal mapping because duration (2.768) is shorter than average life (3) — coupons received along the way shorten the exposure.

Cash flow mapping calculation

The portfolio is broken into five distinct cash flows (years 1–5): Bond 1's coupon-plus-principal in year 1, and Bond 2's coupons in years 1–4 plus its coupon-plus-principal in year 5. Each is discounted to present value and multiplied by the zero-coupon VaR% for its maturity, giving a column of VaR contributions.

Undiversified VaR

If every maturity were perfectly correlated (correlation = 1), portfolio VaR is simply the sum of the individual VaR contributions. Summing the five contributions here gives an undiversified VaR of $2.674 million — the worst case from a diversification standpoint, assuming the worst losses at every maturity happen simultaneously.

Diversified VaR

In reality, zero-coupon returns at different maturities are not perfectly correlated. Diversified VaR accounts for this using the actual correlation matrix, computed via matrix algebra combining the present-value vector, the VaR vector, and the correlation matrix. Here the matrix algebra produces a sum of 6.840 under the square root, giving a diversified VaR of $2.615 million — lower than the undiversified $2.674 million, because the maturities aren't perfectly correlated. The gap between the two is the diversification benefit.

Note: the matrix-algebra calculation behind diversified VaR is too involved for an exam calculator, so it's unlikely to be tested as a full numerical problem — but understanding what undiversified and diversified VaR represent, and that diversified VaR is generally smaller, is essential.
los.formula:
WAL = Σ w_i × maturity_i
Principal mapping VaR = (Total MV) × VaR%(WAL)
Duration = Σ[t × PV(CF_t)] / Σ PV(CF_t)
Duration mapping VaR = (Total MV) × VaR%(Duration)
Undiversified VaR = Σ |x_i| × VaR%_i
Diversified VaR = sqrt(x' R x)
takeaways: Principal mapping VaR ($2.968m) > duration mapping VaR ($2.737m) for the same two-bond portfolio — duration is shorter than average life because coupons shorten exposure.
Undiversified VaR ($2.674m) sums individual maturity VaR contributions assuming correlation = 1.
Diversified VaR ($2.615m) uses the actual correlation matrix and is always ≤ undiversified VaR.
The undiversified/diversified gap is the diversification benefit — it reappears in stress testing, benchmarking, and derivative mapping later in this lesson.

los.lo: LO 5.e — Describe how mapping of risk factors can support stress testing
los.title: Stress Testing via Mapping
los.why: The cash-flow map built for VaR doesn't have to be discarded once VaR is computed — it can be directly reused to stress-test the portfolio. This LO shows that stress testing and undiversified VaR are two routes to the same number, and clarifies exactly when that shortcut is valid.
los.keyIdea: Shocking every mapped cash flow's present value down by its own VaR percentage and revaluing the portfolio reproduces the undiversified VaR — valid only under the perfect-correlation assumption.
los.content:
The idea: revaluation under a uniform shock

Cash flow mapping showed that if all zero-coupon maturities move in perfect lockstep (correlation = 1), portfolio VaR equals the undiversified VaR — the sum of each mapped position's individual VaR. Stress testing offers an alternative route to that same number without summing VaR contributions directly:

  1. Take each mapped zero-coupon position and reduce its value by its own VaR percentage — apply the worst-case loss implied by that maturity's VaR to that cash flow's present value.
  2. Revalue the whole portfolio using these stressed present values.
  3. Compare the stressed value to the original value — the difference is the stress-test loss.

Under perfect correlation, this stress-test loss equals the undiversified VaR from summing individual contributions. The two are mathematically equivalent ways of asking 'what if every position hits its 95% worst case at once' — one sums contributions directly, the other revalues the whole portfolio after applying shocks.

Worked example

Continuing the two-bond portfolio: a 1-year zero discounted at 3.5% has a present value factor of 1/1.035 = 0.9662. Its 95% VaR% is 0.4696%. Applying the shock: stressed PV factor = 0.9662 × (1 − 0.4696/100) = 0.9616.
Applying the same shock-and-revalue process to every maturity's cash flow and summing gives a stressed portfolio value of $197.33 million vs. the original $200.00 million — a difference of $2.67 million, matching the undiversified VaR of $2.674 million from LO 5.d (up to rounding).

Why this is useful — and its limitation

The appeal is simplicity: no correlation matrix to estimate or invert, just a known percentage shock applied to each cash flow followed by ordinary present-value arithmetic. The catch is the assumption it rests on: the shock-and-revalue approach only reproduces the undiversified VaR, valid only if correlations across maturities are exactly 1. As soon as correlations are less than perfect, the true (diversified) VaR is lower, and the stress test overstates actual VaR. This style of stress testing is best understood as a conservative, worst-case benchmark — useful precisely because it's conservative and easy to compute, but not a substitute for diversified VaR when correlations matter.
los.formula:
Stressed PV_t = PV_t × (1 − VaR%_t)
Stress-test loss = (Original portfolio value) − (Stressed portfolio value)
Stress-test loss = Undiversified VaR (only if all correlations = 1)
takeaways: Shock each mapped cash flow's present value down by its own VaR%, revalue the portfolio, and the value drop equals the undiversified VaR — under perfect correlation.
Worked example: stressed value $197.33m vs. original $200.00m → $2.67m loss, matching the $2.674m undiversified VaR.
The big appeal is simplicity — no correlation matrix needed at all.
The result is a conservative worst-case benchmark, not the true (diversified) VaR, whenever correlations are below 1.

los.lo: LO 5.f — Explain how VaR can be computed and used relative to a performance benchmark
los.title: Benchmarking and Tracking Error VaR
los.why: Absolute VaR tells a portfolio manager how risky their portfolio is in isolation, but managers are usually evaluated against a benchmark. Tracking error VaR reframes the mapping machinery to answer a different question — not 'how risky is this portfolio?' but 'how differently does it behave from the benchmark?' — and shows that cash-flow resemblance matters more than matching duration alone.
los.keyIdea: Tracking error VaR is the VaR of the difference between a portfolio's and a benchmark's mapped cash-flow positions — it depends on how closely the cash-flow profiles match, not just on overall duration.
los.content:
Why benchmark relative to VaR?

Two portfolios can share the same absolute VaR yet behave very differently relative to a benchmark, because absolute VaR depends only on overall sensitivity to market moves, while benchmark-relative risk depends on how the portfolio's response pattern differs from the benchmark's, cash flow by cash flow. Tracking error VaR is the VaR of the difference between the managed portfolio and the benchmark — it measures the riskiness of the deviation, not of either portfolio alone.

Worked example setup

A $100 million bond portfolio with duration 4.77 serves as the benchmark. Several candidate portfolios, each just two zero-coupon bonds, are constructed to match this duration.

Step 1 — match duration with two zero-coupon bonds

One candidate combines a 4-year zero (23% weight) with a 5-year zero (77% weight):
0.23 × 4 + 0.77 × 5 = 4.77
This matches the benchmark's duration exactly — even though this candidate's cash flows (concentrated at years 4 and 5) may look very different from the benchmark's actual cash-flow profile (spread across many maturities).

Step 2 — absolute VaR for benchmark and candidates

The benchmark's absolute VaR is computed by applying VaR percentages (for the relevant horizon — here monthly) to its market value weights, coming out to about $1.99 million — close to a 4-year note's VaR%, consistent with the benchmark's duration of 4.77 sitting near 4 years. The same calculation is applied to each two-bond candidate.

Step 3 — tracking error VaR

Tracking error VaR is computed from the difference between the benchmark's market-value position vector (x0) and each candidate's vector (x), run through the same VaR-percentage and correlation-matrix machinery used for diversified VaR.

Interpreting the results

  • Tracking error reflects nonparallel yield-curve shifts. Even though every candidate matches the benchmark's duration, none replicates its cash-flow timing — so when the curve moves non-parallel, candidate and benchmark respond differently. That residual difference is the tracking error. In the example, candidates' TE VaR ranges from about $0.45 million down to about $0.17 million for the best match, all much smaller than the benchmark's $1.99 million absolute VaR.
  • The best-matching candidate is the one whose cash flows most resemble the benchmark's — not the one matching duration most 'elegantly.' The benchmark's cash flows concentrate around the 2-year maturity, so the candidate that itself includes a 2-year zero has the smallest tracking error.
  • Minimizing absolute VaR ≠ minimizing tracking error. A 'barbell' candidate (very short + very long maturities) has the lowest absolute VaR among candidates but the highest tracking error — a portfolio can look attractive stand-alone while being a poor benchmark match.

Variance improvement

Tracking error VaR converts into a measure analogous to R-squared, the variance improvement — the fraction of the benchmark's variance 'explained' by how closely the candidate tracks it. Smaller tracking error relative to benchmark VaR pushes this closer to 100%.

For the best-matching candidate (TE VaR = $0.17 million, Benchmark VaR = $1.99 million):
Variance improvement = 1 − (0.17 / 1.99)² ≈ 99.3%.
los.formula:
TE VaR = VaR(x − x0)
Variance improvement = 1 − (TE VaR / Benchmark VaR)^2
takeaways: Tracking error VaR is the VaR of the difference between a portfolio's and a benchmark's mapped positions, not the VaR of either alone.
Matching duration is not enough — the candidate whose cash-flow profile most resembles the benchmark's has the smallest tracking error.
A barbell portfolio can have the lowest absolute VaR yet the highest tracking error — minimizing one doesn't minimize the other.
Variance improvement = 1 − (TE VaR / Benchmark VaR)^2 ≈ 99.3% for the best-matching candidate in the worked example.

los.lo: LO 5.g — Describe the method of mapping forwards, forward rate agreements, interest rate swaps, and options
los.title: Mapping Forwards, Swaps, and Options
los.why: This LO is where the mapping framework meets derivatives — closing the loop on the lesson. Forwards, FRAs, and swaps decompose cleanly into the same zero-coupon-like building blocks used for fixed-income mapping, so the undiversified/diversified machinery applies directly. Options break the linearity that machinery relies on, which is why this LO ends by flagging the limits of delta-normal mapping.
los.keyIdea: Linear derivatives (forwards, FRAs, swaps) decompose into zero-coupon-bond-like positions and inherit the same undiversified/diversified VaR machinery; options only fit this framework approximately, and that approximation degrades over longer horizons.
los.content:
The delta-normal method, generally

The delta-normal method estimates VaR by treating a portfolio's value as a linear combination of risk factors assumed to be jointly normal. Once expressed this way, the portfolio inherits a correlation matrix from its risk factors, and VaR follows from standard matrix multiplication — the same undiversified/diversified framework from fixed-income mapping. It works best when payoffs really are approximately linear in their risk factors over the relevant horizon — true for forwards, FRAs, and swaps.

Mapping forward contracts

A forward's current value is Forward_t = (F_t − K)e^(−rt). To map it, recognize that a forward to buy a foreign currency one year from now is economically equivalent to holding simultaneously: (1) a short position in a domestic zero-coupon bill, (2) a long position in a foreign-currency zero-coupon bill, and (3) a long spot position in the foreign currency. Each piece is then a position in an instrument the risk system already has volatility/correlation data for.

A representative forward position decomposes into a long foreign-bill position (≈$122.9 million) and an equal-present-value short domestic-bill position. Applying VaR percentages and summing gives an undiversified VaR ≈ $6.01 million; combining the legs via the correlation matrix gives a diversified VaR ≈ $5.59 million — again lower due to imperfect correlation.

This decomposition generalizes: any instrument expressible as a linear combination of basic building blocks (zero-coupon positions, spot FX positions, etc.) can be mapped this way.

Mapping FRAs

An FRA locking in a rate between two future dates (e.g., a '6×12' FRA) is equivalent to borrowing for the near period and lending for the far period (or vice versa). The mapping proceeds by: (1) computing the present value of the notional discounted at the shorter (180-day) rate, (2) deriving the implied forward rate between the 180-day and 360-day points from the two spot rates, (3) treating the position as two offsetting zero-coupon exposures at the 180-day and 360-day points, and (4) computing undiversified VaR as the sum of the two legs' absolute VaR contributions, and diversified VaR via the correlation matrix for the two maturities.

With a 360-day spot rate of 4.5% and a 180-day spot rate of 4.1%: (1 + F/2) = 1.045 / (1 + 0.041/2) → F ≈ 4.8%. PV($100 million notional) = $100m / 1.0205 ≈ $97.991 million. A representative $100 million notional FRA gives an undiversified VaR ≈ $0.62 million and a diversified VaR ≈ $0.35 million.

Mapping interest rate swaps

A swap exchanges fixed for floating cash flows (or vice versa) and decomposes into a fixed-rate bond position plus a floating-rate note position. The fixed leg behaves like a coupon bond maturing at the swap's remaining life; the floating leg, repricing to par at each reset, has present value staying close to its notional.

For a $100 million four-year swap paying fixed and receiving floating: Step 1 — build a table of present values representing a short fixed-leg position (paying fixed = economically short a fixed-coupon bond) out to final maturity, plus a long floating-leg position valued at its notional (≈$100 million). Step 2 — multiply the absolute present-value vector by the VaR percentages for each maturity and sum for undiversified VaR. Step 3 — apply the correlation matrix across maturities to the same vector for diversified VaR, again lower due to imperfect correlation. This is structurally identical to fixed-income cash-flow mapping — the only new step is recognizing the swap's two legs as a short fixed-bond position and a long floating-rate-note position.

Mapping nonlinear derivatives: options

Everything above relies on linearity. An option's value is a nonlinear function of the underlying's price, but for a small enough range, its value change can be approximated as delta × (change in underlying price) — treating the option as a linear position of size 'delta.'

Why this is fragile: the approximation's accuracy depends on delta staying roughly constant. But delta changes as the underlying moves and as time passes (the sensitivity of delta to price is 'gamma'). Over long horizons, delta can change substantially, so today's-delta-based linear approximation becomes inaccurate. Over very short horizons (e.g., one day), delta changes much less, so the linear approximation is much more reasonable — even for options with longer remaining maturities, a one-day delta-normal VaR can be reasonable as long as the horizon stays short.

An option with strike $100 and annualized underlying volatility 25%, restricted to a one-day horizon at 95% confidence: one-day worst-case loss ≈ $2.59, bringing the position to about $100 − $2.59 = $97.41.

Beyond delta-normal: delta-gamma

When the delta-normal approximation isn't good enough — typically longer horizons or options far from linear — a Taylor series approximation including both delta (first-order) and gamma (second-order) terms, the delta-gamma method, captures the curvature delta-normal ignores. The key takeaway: delta-normal mapping is a first approximation valid mainly over short horizons, and delta-gamma is the natural refinement.
los.formula:
Forward_t = (F_t − K) × e^(−rt)
(1 + F_(1,2)/2) = (1 + spot rate to far date) / (1 + spot rate to near date / 2)
PV(notional) = Notional / (1 + near-date spot rate × near-date fraction of year)
One-day loss ≈ (confidence multiplier) × (one-day volatility) × (position value)
takeaways: Forwards, FRAs, and swaps decompose into zero-coupon-bond-like long/short legs that inherit the fixed-income undiversified/diversified VaR machinery directly.
Forward example: undiversified VaR ≈ $6.01m → diversified ≈ $5.59m; FRA example: ≈ $0.62m → ≈ $0.35m — correlation lowers both.
A swap maps to a short fixed-bond leg plus a long floating-rate-note leg (valued near par at each reset).
Delta-normal mapping treats an option as a linear 'delta' position — reasonable over short horizons, unreliable over long ones where delta-gamma is the refinement.
